Parliamentary elections were held in [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary") on 8 April 2018. The elections were the second since the adoption of a new [constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Hungary "Constitution of Hungary"), which came into force on 1 January 2012.

The result was a victory for the [Fidesz](/wiki/Fidesz "Fidesz")–[KDNP](/wiki/Christian_Democratic_People%27s_Party_(Hungary) "Christian Democratic People's Party (Hungary)") alliance, preserving its two-thirds majority, with [Viktor Orbán](/wiki/Viktor_Orb%C3%A1n "Viktor Orbán") remaining Prime Minister. Orbán and Fidesz campaigned primarily on the issues of immigration and foreign meddling, and the election was seen as a victory for [right-wing populism](/wiki/Right-wing_populism "Right-wing populism") in Europe.[[1]](#cite_note-OrbanReuters-6)[[2]](#cite_note-OrbanEUobserver-7)[[3]](#cite_note-MurphyKheraToI-8)

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Hungarian_parliamentary_election&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

At the previous parliamentary election, in April 2014, the incumbent government — composed of [Fidesz](/wiki/Fidesz "Fidesz") and its satellite ally the [KDNP](/wiki/Christian_Democratic_People%27s_Party_(Hungary) "Christian Democratic People's Party (Hungary)") — was able to achieve a two-thirds majority for the second consecutive time with 44.87 percent of the votes. According to their critics, this overwhelming proportion was only because of the new election law (mostly due to the introduction of compensation votes also for the individual winners) which was adopted by the ruling coalition in 2011.[[4]](#cite_note-9) In early 2015, however, Fidesz lost its two-thirds majority following the [2014 Hungarian Internet tax protests](/wiki/2014_Hungarian_Internet_tax_protests "2014 Hungarian Internet tax protests") and subsequent decrease in support for the government.[[5]](#cite_note-10) The governing party suffered defeats at two parliamentary by-elections in February and April 2015, both in [Veszprém County](/wiki/Veszpr%C3%A9m_County "Veszprém County").[[6]](#cite_note-The_Wall_Street_Journal-11)[[7]](#cite_note-Index.hu-12)

The left-wing electoral alliance [Unity](/wiki/Unity_(Hungary) "Unity (Hungary)"), which failed to win the 2014 national election after its five constituent parties gained a total of only 38 seats, broke up shortly thereafter. Its former member parties ([MSZP](/wiki/Hungarian_Socialist_Party "Hungarian Socialist Party"), [Együtt](/wiki/Together_(Hungary) "Together (Hungary)")–[PM](/wiki/Dialogue_for_Hungary "Dialogue for Hungary") and [DK](/wiki/Democratic_Coalition_(Hungary) "Democratic Coalition (Hungary)")) participated in the [May 2014 European Parliament election](/wiki/2014_European_Parliament_election_in_Hungary "2014 European Parliament election in Hungary") individually, while the [MLP](/wiki/Hungarian_Liberal_Party "Hungarian Liberal Party") did not participate in the election at all. Due to this fragmentation of the left-wing opposition, the radical nationalist [Jobbik](/wiki/Jobbik "Jobbik") became the second largest party in a nationwide election for the first time since its establishment.[[8]](#cite_note-13) The PM broke off the permanent nature of its alliance with Együtt on 9 November 2014.[[9]](#cite_note-14)

After a few months of crisis for Fidesz from November 2014, which was marked by internal conflicts (e.g. businessman [Lajos Simicska](/wiki/Lajos_Simicska "Lajos Simicska")'s fall from grace within Fidesz)[[10]](#cite_note-15) and corruption allegations,[[11]](#cite_note-16) the governing party regained much of its lost support during the [European migrant crisis](/wiki/European_migrant_crisis "European migrant crisis") during the summer of 2015, when Prime Minister Viktor Orbán announced the construction of a 4-metre-high (13 ft), 175-kilometre-long (109 mi) [fence along its southern border](/wiki/Hungarian_border_barrier "Hungarian border barrier") with Serbia.[[12]](#cite_note-17) The Hungarian government also criticised the official [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") policy for not dissuading migrants from entering Europe.[[13]](#cite_note-18) The barrier became successful, as from 17 October 2015 onward, thousands of migrants were diverted daily to Slovenia instead.[[14]](#cite_note-SloveniaCroatia2-19)

On 13 December 2015, the 26th congress of the ruling Fidesz re-elected Viktor Orbán as party leader. Orbán said in his speech that he was ready to lead the party into the forthcoming parliamentary election and to continue to serve as prime minister if Fidesz won re-election in 2018. With that statement, Orbán made clear that he did not intend to become [President of Hungary](/wiki/List_of_heads_of_state_of_Hungary "List of heads of state of Hungary") in succession to [János Áder](/wiki/J%C3%A1nos_%C3%81der "János Áder") during the 2017 indirect presidential election.[[15]](#cite_note-20)

On 2 October 2017, the elected leader of the MSZP, [László Botka](/wiki/L%C3%A1szl%C3%B3_Botka "László Botka"), announced his withdrawal, saying that he thought some of the Hungarian opposition did not care about changing government.[[16]](#cite_note-21)

Orbán and Fidesz's strength going into the election came into question when the party unexpectedly lost a mayoral by-election in [Hódmezővásárhely](/wiki/H%C3%B3dmez%C5%91v%C3%A1s%C3%A1rhely "Hódmezővásárhely"), considered a Fidesz stronghold, on 25 February 2018, to an [independent candidate](/wiki/P%C3%A9ter_M%C3%A1rki-Zay "Péter Márki-Zay") supported by every opposition party.[[17]](#cite_note-22)[[18]](#cite_note-23)[[19]](#cite_note-24) Election observers and critics of Orbán speculated whether Hungary's opposition parties could create a similar alliance on the national level,[[20]](#cite_note-25)[[21]](#cite_note-26) though the opposition parties had been unable to create a common strategy by late March 2018.[[22]](#cite_note-27) Orbán increased his efforts as a result of this loss.[[23]](#cite_note-28)

According to observers prior to the election, winning re-election was seen as more difficult for Orbán than expected.[[24]](#cite_note-29)

## Electoral system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Hungarian_parliamentary_election&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Electoral system")]

Main article: [Electoral system of Hungary](/wiki/Electoral_system_of_Hungary "Electoral system of Hungary")

The 199 members of the [National Assembly](/wiki/National_Assembly_(Hungary) "National Assembly (Hungary)") were to be elected by two methods; 106 would be elected in single-member constituencies by [first-past-the-post voting](/wiki/First-past-the-post_voting "First-past-the-post voting"), with the remaining 93 elected from a single nationwide constituency mostly by [proportional representation](/wiki/Proportional_representation "Proportional representation"), via a [partially compensatory system](/wiki/Scorporo "Scorporo") (a hybrid of [parallel voting](/wiki/Parallel_voting "Parallel voting") and the [mixed single vote](/wiki/Mixed_single_vote "Mixed single vote")). The electoral threshold was set at 5%, although this was raised to 10% for coalitions of two parties and 15% for coalitions of three or more parties. Seats were to be allocated using the [d'Hondt method](/wiki/D%27Hondt_method "D'Hondt method").[[25]](#cite_note-30)

Since 2014, each of the Armenian, Bulgarian, Croatian, German, Greek, Polish, Romani, Romanian, Rusyn, Serbian, Slovakian, Slovenian, and Ukrainian ethnic minorities can win one of the 93 party lists seats if they register as a specific lists and reach a lowered quota of ![{\displaystyle {\frac {1}{4\times 93}}={\frac {1}{372}}\approx 0.2688\%}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/cccfc04d31a0840580fcf238c299b790b41cfc65) of the total of party list votes.[[26]](#cite_note-Nemzeti_Választási_Iroda-31) Each minority is able to send a minority spokesman – without the rights of an MP – to the National Assembly, if the list does not reach this lowered quota.[[26]](#cite_note-Nemzeti_Választási_Iroda-31)

Orbán was congratulated by [German Chancellor](/wiki/Chancellor_of_Germany "Chancellor of Germany") [Angela Merkel](/wiki/Angela_Merkel "Angela Merkel"),[[51]](#cite_note-EUfar-right-56) [Polish Prime Babiš](/wiki/Andrej_Babi%C5%A1 "Andrej Babiš"),[[52]](#cite_note-ShotterFT-57) [Israeli Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Israel "Prime Minister of Israel") [Benjamin Netanyahu](/wiki/Benjamin_Netanyahu "Benjamin Netanyahu"),[[53]](#cite_note-58)[[54]](#cite_note-59) [European Council President](/wiki/President_of_the_European_Council "President of the European Council") [Donald Tusk](/wiki/Donald_Tusk "Donald Tusk"),[[55]](#cite_note-60) British foreign minister [Boris Johnson](/wiki/Boris_Johnson "Boris Johnson")[[56]](#cite_note-61) and Former Canadian Pen](/wiki/Marine_Le_Pen "Marine Le Pen"), [Geert Wilders](/wiki/Geert_Wilders "Geert Wilders"), [Beatrix von Storch](/wiki/Beatrix_von_Storch "Beatrix von Storch"), Vice Chancellor of victory.[[51]](#cite_note-EUfar-right-56)[[58]](#cite_note-63)[[59]](#cite_note-64)[[60]](#cite_note-65)[[61]](#cite_note-ChaseDW-66) German Interior Minister [Horst Seehofer](/wiki/Horst_Seehofer ### Analysis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Hungarian_parliamentary_election&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Analysis")]

According to *[The Washington Post](/wiki/The_Washington_Post "The Washington Post")* the election was "easily the most consequential since Hungary’s post-communist transition", and it "represented a victory for the European far right".[[70]](#cite_note-McAuleyWaPo-75) Orbán campaigned exclusively on his opposition to immigration and foreign meddling[[1]](#cite_note-OrbanReuters-6)[[2]](#cite_note-OrbanEUobserver-7)[[3]](#cite_note-MurphyKheraToI-8)[[71]](#cite_note-76)[[72]](#cite_note-BayerPolitico-77)[[73]](#cite_note-78) and his victory was seen as a boost for his [Eurosceptic](/wiki/Eurosceptic "Eurosceptic") and nationalist policies as well as for other [right-wing populist](/wiki/Right-wing_populist "Right-wing populist") governments and political parties across Europe, such as in Austria and Poland.[[1]](#cite_note-OrbanReuters-6)[[2]](#cite_note-OrbanEUobserver-7)[[3]](#cite_note-MurphyKheraToI-8)[[61]](#cite_note-ChaseDW-66)[[72]](#cite_note-BayerPolitico-77) The election results strengthened Orbán's position over Hungarian politics,[[74]](#cite_note-79)[[75]](#cite_note-BuckleyFT-80) giving his party the ability to change Hungary's constitution again,[[76]](#cite_note-81) and they were seen as a setback to the European Union along with a string of other elections throughout Europe.[[77]](#cite_note-FiveThings-82)[[78]](#cite_note-83)

The election saw a large surge in voter turnout, one of the largest in post-communist Hungarian history,[[77]](#cite_note-FiveThings-82) which benefited Fidesz despite pre-election expectations that it would help the opposition.[[2]](#cite_note-OrbanEUobserver-7)[[3]](#cite_note-MurphyKheraToI-8)[[75]](#cite_note-BuckleyFT-80)[[79]](#cite_note-CsakyFP-84) Fidesz significantly outperformed its election result expectations,[[79]](#cite_note-CsakyFP-84)[[80]](#cite_note-85)[[81]](#cite_note-86) but was reported to have lost support among younger voters.[[82]](#cite_note-BBC_9_Apr_2018-87) There was also a geographical split in the results, with opposition parties winning the majority of seats in Budapest, while provincial towns and rural areas were predominantly won by the Fidesz coalition.[[75]](#cite_note-BuckleyFT-80)[[82]](#cite_note-BBC_9_Apr_2018-87) Despite this, *The Washington Post* described the results as "a crushing defeat for left-leaning opposition leaders".[[70]](#cite_note-McAuleyWaPo-75)

According to Zselyke Csaky of *[Foreign Policy](/wiki/Foreign_Policy "Foreign Policy")*, Orbán won partially because of a growing Hungarian economy, his centralization of power over the previous eight years, and "the brutally effective propaganda campaign he has waged against all enemies".[[79]](#cite_note-CsakyFP-84) According to Shaun Walker of *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")*, Orbán's opposition to immigration and "a coordinated, expensive and sophisticated sting operation" by the Hungarian government on various NGOs contributed to his victory.[[83]](#cite_note-88)

The election was also notable for seeing a representative of [Hungary's German minority](/wiki/Germans_of_Hungary "Germans of Hungary") be elected for the first time since 1933.[[77]](#cite_note-FiveThings-82)[[84]](#cite_note-89)

### Electoral conduct

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Hungarian_parliamentary_election&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Electoral conduct")]

A preliminary report on the election by the [Organization for Security and Co-operation in Europe](/wiki/Organization_for_Security_and_Co-operation_in_Europe "Organization for Security and Co-operation in Europe") (OSCE) criticised the electoral conduct and stated that Fidesz used government resources for its election campaign.[[85]](#cite_note-90) A spokesman described campaigning language as "quite hostile and xenophobic".[[86]](#cite_note-Guardian_OSCE-91) The report criticised the atmosphere as limiting wide-ranging debate and found that public television broadcasts were biased towards the governing coalition. It also criticised the use of "information campaigns" funded out of public money, which it stated generated "a pervasive overlap between state and ruling party resources, undermining contestants' ability to compete on an equal basis".[[86]](#cite_note-Guardian_OSCE-91)

## Government formation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Hungarian_parliamentary_election&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Government formation")]

Main article: [Fourth Orbán Government](/wiki/Fourth_Orb%C3%A1n_Government "Fourth Orbán Government")

On 12 April 2018, cabinet member [Antal Rogán](/wiki/Antal_Rog%C3%A1n "Antal Rogán") announced [TV2](/wiki/TV2_(Hungary) "TV2 (Hungary)") that "there will be a new government formed, mostly with new members and a new structure", in line with the government's new priorities, including demography and family policy and migrant issue.[[87]](#cite_note-92) On 20 April, Orbán said "I would say that people not voted for the continuation of the work of the present government, but they want no change in the service of the goals". There he announced the replacement of [János Lázár](/wiki/J%C3%A1nos_L%C3%A1z%C3%A1r "János Lázár") as Minister of the Prime Minister's Office, one of the most influential members of his former cabinets, and confirmed [Mihály Varga](/wiki/Mih%C3%A1ly_Varga "Mihály Varga") will remain minister responsible for economy. He called solving demographic problems as the government's most important task.[[88]](#cite_note-93) On 23 April, [Zoltán Balog](/wiki/Zolt%C3%A1n_Balog_(politician) "Zoltán Balog (politician)") announced, he will step down as Minister of Human Resources, disagreeing with the prime minister over structural considerations, who intended to keep the type of superministry system. Balog will lead the Fidesz's Foundation for Civic Hungary.[[89]](#cite_note-94) Next day, [Sándor Fazekas](/wiki/S%C3%A1ndor_Fazekas "Sándor Fazekas") also said that he will not continue his work as Minister of Agriculture after eight years.[[90]](#cite_note-95) On 25 April, minister without portfolio [Lajos Kósa](/wiki/Lajos_K%C3%B3sa "Lajos Kósa") announced he will leave the government, as Orbán entrusted him to lead the Fidesz campaign for the upcoming local elections in 2019.[[91]](#cite_note-96) On the same day, the online version of [ATV](/wiki/ATV_(Hungary) "ATV (Hungary)") reported that [Minister of Defence](/wiki/Minister_of_Defence_(Hungary) "Minister of Defence (Hungary)") [István Simicskó](/wiki/Istv%C3%A1n_Simicsk%C3%B3 "István Simicskó") also left the government, and will be replaced by "a general".[[92]](#cite_note-97)

Senior press officer Bertalan Havasi announced the compilation of the government on 27 April, which largely confirmed previous unofficial information. Accordingly, the name of the Ministry of National Economy was restored to Ministry of Finance, which existed before 2010.[[93]](#cite_note-Orban4Gov-98) The Ministry of National Development abolished and its duties were distributed: [Andrea Bártfai-Mager](/wiki/Andrea_B%C3%A1rtfai-Mager "Andrea Bártfai-Mager") was appointed minister without portfolio for managing national wealth and [László Palkovics](/wiki/L%C3%A1szl%C3%B3_Palkovics "László Palkovics") became head of the newly formed Ministry of National Innovation and Technology, while energy issues were assigned to the Prime Minister's Office.[[94]](#cite_note-99) The Ministry of Agriculture was renamed from "Földművelésügyi Minisztérium" to "Agrárminisztérium" with no change in the scope of operations.[[95]](#cite_note-100) Seven members of Orbán's third cabinet – [Zsolt Semjén](/wiki/Zsolt_Semj%C3%A9n "Zsolt Semjén"), Antal Rogán, [Péter Szijjártó](/wiki/P%C3%A9ter_Szijj%C3%A1rt%C3%B3 "Péter Szijjártó"), [Sándor Pintér](/wiki/S%C3%A1ndor_Pint%C3%A9r "Sándor Pintér"), [László Trócsányi](/wiki/L%C3%A1szl%C3%B3_Tr%C3%B3cs%C3%A1nyi "László Trócsányi"), Mihály Varga and [János Süli](/wiki/J%C3%A1nos_S%C3%BCli "János Süli") – retained their positions. [Miklós Kásler](/wiki/Mikl%C3%B3s_K%C3%A1sler "Miklós Kásler"), the Director of the National Institute of Oncology was appointed Minister of Human Resources, while incumbent Secretary of State for Agriculture [István Nagy](/wiki/Istv%C3%A1n_Nagy_(politician,_1967) "István Nagy (politician, 1967)") replaced his superior Sándor Fazekas.[[93]](#cite_note-Orban4Gov-98) Fidesz caucus leader [Gergely Gulyás](/wiki/Gergely_Guly%C3%A1s "Gergely Gulyás") became the new Minister of the Prime Minister's Office, but the evolving Prime Minister's Government Office under the direct management of Viktor Orbán has reduced his powers.[[96]](#cite_note-101) Col. Gen. [Tibor Benkő](/wiki/Tibor_Benk%C5%91_(military_officer) "Tibor Benkő (military officer)"), who has served as [Chief of the General Staff](/wiki/General_Staff_of_the_Armed_Forces_of_the_Republic_of_Hungary "General Staff of the Armed Forces of the Republic of Hungary") since 2010 was nominated to the position of Minister of Defence, becoming the first active military officer since the [end of communism](/wiki/End_of_communism_in_Hungary_(1989) "End of communism in Hungary (1989)"), who held the ministry.[[97]](#cite_note-102)

On 10 May, the new Hungarian Parliament elected Orbán to a fourth term.[[98]](#cite_note-103) Orbán's new cabinet was then sworn in on 18 May.[[99]](#cite_note-104)
